title: ULF: UNSUPERVISED LABELING FUNCTION CORRECTION USING CROSS-VALIDATION FOR WEAK SUPERVISION

structured.summary: The paper proposes ULF, an unsupervised method to improve weak supervision label quality by refining the labeling function (LF)-to-class assignment matrix through LF signature-based cross-validation. ULF leverages LF structure to identify and correct LF-specific noise without requiring labeled data, demonstrating superior performance over existing WS denoising methods on multiple NLP datasets.

strengths.point: Novel use of LF signature-based data splitting for cross-validation denoising
strengths.evidence: ULF splits data by LF matches rather than randomly, enabling better identification of LF-specific noise and improved LF-to-class assignment refinement.

strengths.point: Unsupervised refinement of LF-to-class assignment matrix
strengths.evidence: ULF recalibrates the LF-to-class matrix T using out-of-sample predictions and confidence thresholds, allowing soft and more accurate LF label assignments without manual annotations.

strengths.point: Strong empirical performance across multiple datasets and languages
strengths.evidence: ULF outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on 4 out of 6 datasets, including English and African language text classification tasks, demonstrating robustness and general applicability.

strengths.point: Compatibility with any classifier and handling of unlabeled samples
strengths.evidence: ULF uses logistic regression with TF-IDF features in experiments but is classifier-agnostic and includes a strategy to label samples without LF matches, which are typically discarded.
The method is implemented within the Knodle framework, facilitating reproducibility and further research.

weaknesses.point: Lack of comparison to recent LF denoising or quality estimation methods
weaknesses.evidence: The paper compares only to Snorkel-DP and FlyingSquid but omits methods like Coral or other recent approaches modeling LF accuracies or dependencies.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient theoretical or empirical justification for signature-based splitting
weaknesses.evidence: While signature splitting is central, the paper lacks ablation or analysis demonstrating why it better identifies LF noise compared to other stratifications.

weaknesses.point: Ad hoc handling of samples without LF matches
weaknesses.evidence: Randomly assigning labels to a small portion of unmatched samples is not well justified or analyzed for its impact on noise or bias.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation metrics and statistical rigor
weaknesses.evidence: Results report only accuracy without additional metrics like F1 or confidence intervals; no statistical significance testing is provided.

weaknesses.point: Reproducibility hindered by missing hyperparameter and training details
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not specify logistic regression hyperparameters, random seeds, or detailed training procedures.

weaknesses.point: Unclear description of confidence thresholding and calibration steps
weaknesses.evidence: Procedures for computing confidence thresholds and calibrating the confidence matrix are ambiguous, limiting reproducibility.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of computational complexity or scalability
weaknesses.evidence: ULF involves k-fold cross-validation with multiple training steps, but runtime or resource requirements are not addressed.

weaknesses.point: Limited generalizability beyond NLP and moderate LF set sizes
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments focus on text classification with 6-18 LFs; applicability to other modalities or very large/small LF sets is untested.

questions.question: How does ULF perform when the majority of LFs are highly noisy or conflicting?
questions.motivation: Understanding robustness to poor LF quality is critical since many real-world WS scenarios involve unreliable LFs.

questions.question: Can the signature-based splitting strategy be theoretically justified or improved?
questions.motivation: A deeper understanding or alternative stratifications could enhance noise detection and correction efficacy.

questions.question: What is the impact of the fraction of randomly labeled unmatched samples on final performance?
questions.motivation: This ad hoc step may introduce bias or noise; quantifying its effect is important for method reliability.

questions.question: How does ULF scale computationally with large datasets or many LFs?
questions.motivation: Practical deployment requires knowledge of runtime and resource demands, especially given the cross-validation approach.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of comparison to recent LF denoising or quality estimation methods. The paper compares only to Snorkel-DP and FlyingSquid but omits methods like Coral or other recent approaches modeling LF accuracies or dependencies
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient theoretical or empirical justification for signature-based splitting. While signature splitting is central, the paper lacks ablation or analysis demonstrating why it better identifies LF noise compared to other stratifications
critique_points.point_003: Ad hoc handling of samples without LF matches. Randomly assigning labels to a small portion of unmatched samples is not well justified or analyzed for its impact on noise or bias
critique_points.point_004: Limited evaluation metrics and statistical rigor. Results report only accuracy without additional metrics like F1 or confidence intervals; no statistical significance testing is provided
critique_points.point_005: Reproducibility hindered by missing hyperparameter and training details. The paper does not specify logistic regression hyperparameters, random seeds, or detailed training procedures
critique_points.point_006: Unclear description of confidence thresholding and calibration steps. Procedures for computing confidence thresholds and calibrating the confidence matrix are ambiguous, limiting reproducibility
critique_points.point_007: No discussion of computational complexity or scalability. ULF involves k-fold cross-validation with multiple training steps, but runtime or resource requirements are not addressed
critique_points.point_008: Limited generalizability beyond NLP and moderate LF set sizes. Experiments focus on text classification with 6-18 LFs; applicability to other modalities or very large/small LF sets is untested
